notes: The ED-bay intent, simulated EHR, live dialogue, and time-boxed trace controls are visible, but the scene still reads as a training scaffold rather than a realistic emergency department bay.
The patient is upright as a capsule placeholder rather than visibly distressed on the bed with exam posture, gowning, monitoring leads, pain behavior, and team positioning.
Patient, nurse, and spouse remain capsule-and-head placeholders without faces, hands, clothing, skin detail, rigging, gaze, gesture, or pain response.

Equipment is sparse and abstract; the scene needs monitor detail, ECG/lead setup, oxygen/IV affordances, bedside furniture, room surfaces, and asset provenance before it can support high-stakes realism claims.
The right-side administrative panel is readable on desktop, and the in-scene Simulated EHR and Live Dialogue panels are mostly legible.
The in-scene Input Evidence panel is too small in this capture for reliable adversarial review, and desktop readability is not evidence of in-headset readability.
Trace buttons are visible in the desktop panel, but WebXR is unavailable in this capture and no controller, hand-select, locomotion, or immersive entry interaction is proven.
The page evidence reports preview frames only and no hand input, which matches a desktop preview posture rather than Quest runtime behavior.
The overall spatial layout is understandable, but actor scale, bed placement, wall clock position, and panel depth need headset and IWER visual checks to confirm comfort and occlusion.
The foreground bed blocks the lower scene, while the side panel consumes substantial viewport space; this is acceptable for desktop review but not proof of XR framing.
This artifact is useful for adversarial visual iteration only.
It does not prove Quest foreground performance, immersive session entry, hand-tracking quality, controller latency, text readability in headset, thermal behavior, or production runtime readiness.
